title: Bulbophyllum pelma
intro: Bulbophyllum pelma, also known as the Pelma orchid, is a species of orchid native to Madagascar, characterized by its unique, long-lasting flowers. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, thanks to its potential anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antifungal properties. The bioactive constituents of Bulbophyllum pelma include flavonoids, phenolic compounds, and alkaloids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often used to prepare infusions, decoctions, and extracts, which are commonly used to treat infections, wounds, and other health conditions.
taxonomy: Bulbophyllum pelma is classified within the family Orchidaceae, which is the largest family of flowering plants. This family is further divided into various subfamilies, with Bulbophyllum being part of the Epidendroideae subfamily. The genus Bulbophyllum itself is one of the largest genera of orchids, comprising over 2,000 species. Bulbophyllum pelma, like its close relatives, is characterized by its unique growth habits and often epiphytic or terrestrial nature. The exact classification of Bulbophyllum pelma can be further refined based on its specific characteristics and phylogenetic relationships.
